name: Candrakirti
dates_notes: Candrakīrti is conventionally placed in the seventh century on the basis of his explicit polemical engagement with Bhāviveka (c. 500–570 CE) and the absence of any reference to him in pre-650 sources. Tibetan hagiography places him at Nālandā; the Indian textual evidence is silent on his life. Lindtner (in Lindtner 1979 and elsewhere) accepts c. 600–650 CE; MacDonald (In Clear Words: The Prasannapadā, Chapter One, 2015) follows the same window.

lineage_polemical.note: Candrakīrti's Prasanna-Padā opens with a sustained refutation of Bhāviveka's use of svatantra-anumāna in commenting on the MMK: an independent inference cannot be admitted at the conventional level without smuggling in a position the Mādhyamaka must refuse, and only prasaṅga-style consequence-refutation preserves Nāgārjuna's na me kācana pratijñā asti commitment. The Prāsaṅgika / Svātantrika distinction is generated by this disagreement.

Śaṅkara's BSB 2.2.32 dismissal of the śūnyavādin position applies in particular to the Prāsaṅgika construal: the refusal even of conventional posit makes the position, on Śaṅkara's reading, even less compatible with the Vedāntic affirmation of brahman as the satya-jagat-kāraṇa than the Sākāra Yogācāra positions are.

Madhva's anti-Mādhyamaka polemic targets the Prāsaṅgika position as the limit-case: a position that refuses every conventional ontology cannot ground bheda, sākṣi-pratyakṣa, or the svatantra-paratantra hierarchy that Tattvavāda makes constitutive of reality.

core_thesis: Candrakīrti is the founder of *Prāsaṅgika Mādhyamaka*: the strict reading of Nāgārjuna on which the middle way refuses every positive ontological commitment, even at the conventional (*saṃvṛti*) level. The contrast is with Bhāviveka's *Svātantrika*, which had proposed that *svatantra-anumāna* — independent positive inference — is admissible at the conventional level without compromising ultimate emptiness. Candrakīrti's reply, developed across the *Prasanna-Padā* (commentary on MMK) and the *Madhyamakāvatāra* with its auto-commentary, is that any *svatantra-anumāna* requires both parties to share a positive ontology of the *pakṣa*, the *hetu*, and the *vyāpti* — and the Mādhyamaka by definition does not. The only legitimate Mādhyamaka argument-form is *prasaṅga*: drawing out the consequences of the opponent's own commitments to show that they undermine themselves. *Saṃvṛti-satya* is preserved as everyday usage that the philosopher does not need to vindicate metaphysically; *paramārtha* is the simple absence of *svabhāva*, not a higher reality. The position has the radical consequence that even the *abhidharma* analysis of *dharmas* must be refused at the ultimate level: there are no minimal phenomenal constituents, only conventional designations.

engaged_works.title: Prasanna-Pada
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; survives complete in Sanskrit (the only complete Sanskrit Mādhyamaka commentary on the MMK).

ascription_evidence.description: MacDonald 2015; de la Vallée Poussin 1903–1913 critical edition.

engaged_works.summary: The principal Mādhyamaka commentary on the MMK, structured chapter-by-chapter to follow Nāgārjuna's twenty-seven *prakaraṇa*s. The opening chapter is the load-bearing one: Candrakīrti uses it to refute Bhāviveka's *svatantra-anumāna* methodology, arguing that the only Mādhyamaka argument-form compatible with *na me kācana pratijñā asti* is *prasaṅga* — consequence-refutation that draws out the unwanted implications of the opponent's commitments without taking on positive commitments of one's own. The remaining chapters provide the Prāsaṅgika reading of Nāgārjuna, with a sustained engagement with the Yogācāra position (especially *vijñapti-mātratā*) which Candrakīrti rejects as a substitute *svabhāva* in epistemic disguise. The text is the principal source for the Prāsaṅgika–Svātantrika distinction and is the most complete Sanskrit Mādhyamaka commentary surviving from the Indian tradition.

engaged_works.title: Madhyamakavatara
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored; preserved in Sanskrit (chapter 6 fully) and complete in Tibetan, with Candrakīrti's own auto-commentary the Madhyamakāvatāra-Bhāṣya.

ascription_evidence.description: Tibetan tradition treats the text as foundational; Sanskrit chapter-six fragments confirm Candrakīrti's authorship.

engaged_works.summary: An independent treatise organised around the ten *bhūmi*s of the bodhisattva path, with the sixth chapter (*abhimukhī-bhūmi*, on the perfection of *prajñā*) supplying the philosophical centre — over two hundred verses developing the Prāsaṅgika position, refuting the Yogācāra *vijñapti-mātratā* position, and treating the two-truths in detail. Chapter 6 contains the famous chariot-analysis of the self in seven points (which Tsong-kha-pa later canonises as the standard Mādhyamaka selflessness-argument) and the explicit defence of *saṃvṛti-satya* as deceptive-yet-functional designation. The text is the bridge between the technical *Prasanna-Padā* and the soteriological-praxis frame of bodhisattva-mārga.

reception_notes: Candrakīrti's reception in Indian Vedāntic discourse is filtered through Bhāviveka and the BSB-anchored polemical chain — the Prāsaṅgika position is engaged as the limit-case of the *śūnyavādin* refusal of any conventional ontology. The Prāsaṅgika–Svātantrika distinction itself only crystallises as a category in the Tibetan *grub-mtha'* literature, but the underlying disagreement on *svatantra-anumāna* is already operative in the seventh-century Indian sources.
